Name: Frangipani: The Short Film
Review:
I have always loved emotional storytelling and listening to people. That is probably why Frangipani connected with me so deeply. It starts as a simple song but slowly turns into something emotionally layered, philosophical, painful, and strangely healing. It never feels like a fictional story being narrated to us rather it feels like a slice of our own life stitched together through music. One of the greatest strengths of Frangipani is Kaber’s writing. The lyrics use casual colloquial language, yet carry emotional depth and philosophical weight that linger in our minds even after the song moves past those lines. The brilliance lies in how naturally these thoughts are presented. The song never pauses to announce how profound it is being. It simply says something deeply human and quietly moves on, trusting the listener to carry the emotional weight. My favorite lines in the song are:
பழைய நட்பு காஞ்ச சணல் போல... ஒரு பொறி பட்டாலே பத்திக்கும் அல்லவா...
அவள் ஒன்னும் புனிதமில்ல... அட மனிதரில் யாரை புனிதம் சொல்ல!!
These lines hold universal truths that only life experience can fully help us understand. The comparison of friendship to a dried rope that can instantly catch fire perfectly captures how certain bonds never truly disappear, no matter how much time or distance passes. And the line about no human being pure refuses to view people as perfection. Instead, it accepts human flaws while still recognizing the light someone can bring into the lives of others. That emotional maturity is what makes Frangipani feel so honest. To me, Nila never felt like just a single character. She reminded me of different people I have crossed paths with in life. Especially my late school friend Misbah (RIP). We had known each other for a long time before I left town after my 10th grade. As months passed, I slowly lost regular contact with many of my friends. One day, a friend told me that Misbah had been diagnosed with a terminal illness and had already passed away. By the time I came to know about it, everything was already over. Hearing that news left me disturbed in a way I could not fully process back then. Misbah and I may not have had long or deep conversations, but we shared those innocent little interactions that only children can have. Over time, I moved on with life, or at least I thought I did. Then came the Frangipani. The song became like a trigger switch for emotions I had unknowingly buried deep within myself. Listening to it felt overwhelming at times, almost left me in a trance state. At first, it reminded me only of pain and grief. But slowly, that same pain started comforting me. Somehow, the sorrow became so emotionally honest that it almost started healing the wounds it reopened. That is why I believe Frangipani resonates with so many people. Nila is not just “Nila.” Everyone who listens to this song sees someone from their own life within Nila, a friend, a memory, a lost connection, an unfinished conversation, or a person who once brought light into our lives and quietly disappeared from it. Frangipani is not just a song about one character; it is a slice of all our lives, directly or indirectly. Because of how personal the song felt to me, I always had my own visuals in mind whenever I listened to it. I strongly believe that is the beauty of Frangipani. Every listener imagines their own world while hearing it. So when the short film adaptation was announced, I was excited. But as the release date got closer, I also became strangely nervous. Not because I feared they would fail the adaptation, but because I feared losing my own imagination of the song. I worried that once the visuals were fixed in front of me, I would never again experience the song in the same personal way. The closest way I can describe that feeling is - it felt like someone going to confess their love to a person they deeply care about and are almost certain loves them back. But there is some trembling reluctance and it does not come from fear of rejection, but from the awareness that once those feelings are spoken aloud, the relationship may never remain the same again. That was exactly how I felt about the short film. When it finally released, I tried watching it multiple times, but each time I closed my eyes and simply listened to the soundtrack instead. For almost two months, that became my routine. Somewhere deep inside, I was afraid to confront the visuals directly. But eventually, I gathered the courage to finally watch it fully. And It felt so great confessing my love for Frangipani. And the result was nothing like the fear I had imagined. Marun and the team did a beautiful job of translating the emotional essence of Frangipani into visuals without taking away the personal connection towards the song. The cinematography and storytelling gently complemented the lyrics. Marun and Team didn't shatter my imagination, rather gave some more life to it. Frangipani is not just relatable because it talks about grief. It is relatable because it understands human connection in all its fragile and imperfect beauty. It understands old friendships, emotional distance, flaws, memory, absence, and the strange way pain sometimes transforms itself into healing. Some songs are heard. Some songs are felt. Frangipani is better lived.
